title: About Me
text:
Hobbies
I am a super crafty person! My grandma taught me to sew and crochet at a young age, and I never stopped.
coloring, baking, and
Favorite things
I love cats, gingham, the color pink,
Goals
Travels
photo
Last summer, I was fortunate to have the opportunity to study abroad in Barcelona, Spain. I tested my high school Spanish language proficiency and took classes in Spanish during the week, then explored the region on the weekends. I especially enjoyed a trip to Venice, Italy, where I then took the train to Lake Garda.
I also recently traveled to Portland, Oregon and witnessed the beauty of their spring bloom.

title: How to Generate Load Profiles with ResStock and ComStock
text:
ResStock time series graph example.


In January 2026, I began interning as as a Programs Associate at the Clean Coalition. My work includes conducting site-specific Solar Microgrid Feasibility Studies and collaborating with a variety of organizations to accelerate the transition to renewable energy.
My first blog post “How to Generate Load Profiles with ResStock and ComStock” describes the process of using the residential and commercial building model databases, ResStock and ComStock, to complete the Load Profiles portion of the Clean Coalition’s Solar Microgrid Methodology (SMM).

title: Stella Symonds
text: Hi! My name is Stella Symonds and I am from Orinda, CA. I am in my third year at UCSB pursuing a B.S. in Environmental Studies with a concentration in Statistics. This website serves as a personal portfolio. Please reach out via email if you’d like to connect!

title: ENV S 116 Final Project: Disproportionality
text:
Disproportionality.


I created a tapestry that depicts the concept of disproportionality and its roots in the social inequalities that our systems are built on. On the left side representing the privileged few, two people stand on a pyramid of money. Their river is healthy and bright blue and they have a living tree and shrubs, since the pollution they create and profit off of is affecting the other side of the land. They can look up and see stars in the night sky. The pollution they create barely degrades their part of the world, so they don’t feel a need to change how they live. Their high-polluting buildings and factories are purposefully placed as close to low-income and predominantly POC neighborhoods as possible. On the right side depicting the majority of the population, their stars in the night sky are replaced with a cloud of gray. Instead of a pyramid of money, the people have built scaffolding to bring them up to the same height as the privileged few. I used a dark red color for the ground to provoke feelings of urgency and doom from the viewer. I wanted the megaphone to stand out and represent urgency, so I wanted it to be the only part of the tapestry in white and bright red. It is up to the viewer whether the privileged few will choose to listen and change their ways or continue to turn a blind eye to the majority of the population’s suffering. In the spirit of sustainability, my tapestry is made completely of secondhand or scrap materials I have collected or been donated over my time as a seamstress, with a significant portion being both secondhand and scrap material. Even the thread is secondhand! When I create, whether that be with sewing or other methods, I do it as sustainably as I can. In my mind, sewing with secondhand fabric makes the process even more special, as the fabric itself has a story behind it.
My tapestry was later featured in a soecial issue of a magazine and in x.
